candidates.text: There's a new kind of coding I call "vibe coding", where you fully give in to the vibes, embrace exponentials, and forget that the code even exists. It's possible because the LLMs (e.g. Cursor Composer w Sonnet) are getting too good. Also I just talk to Composer with SuperWhisper so I barely even touch the keyboard. I ask for the dumbest things like "decrease the padding on the sidebar by half" because I'm too lazy to find it. I "Accept All" always, I don't read the diffs anymore. When I get error messages I just copy paste them in with no comment, usually that fixes it. The code grows beyond my usual comprehension, I'd have to really read through it for a while. Sometimes the LLMs can't fix a bug so I just work around it or ask for random changes until it goes away. It's not too bad for throwaway weekend projects, but still quite amusing. I'm building a project or webapp, but it's not really coding - I just see stuff, say stuff, run stuff, and copy paste stuff, and it mostly works.

Noticing myself adopting a certain rhythm in AI-assisted coding (i.e. code I actually and professionally care about, contrast to vibe code).

1. Stuff everything relevant into context (this can take a while in big projects. If the project is small enough just stuff everything e.g. `files-to-prompt . -e ts -e tsx -e css -e md --cxml --ignore node_modules -o prompt.xml`)
2. Describe the next single, concrete incremental change we're trying to implement. Don't ask for code, ask for a few high-level approaches, pros/cons. There's almost always a few ways to do thing and the LLM's judgement is not always great. Optionally make concrete.
3. Pick one approach, ask for first draft code.
4. Review / learning phase: (Manually...) pull up all the API docs in a side browser of functions I haven't called before or I am less familiar with, ask for explanations, clarifications, changes, wind back and try a different approach.
6. Test.
7. Git commit.
Ask for suggestions on what we could implement next. Repeat.

Something like this feels more along the lines of the inner loop of AI-assisted development. The emphasis is on keeping a very tight leash on this new over-eager junior intern savant with encyclopedic knowledge of software, but who also bullshits you all the time, has an over-abundance of courage and shows little to no taste for good code. And emphasis on being slow, defensive, careful, paranoid, and on always taking the inline learning opportunity, not delegating. Many of these stages are clunky and manual and aren't made explicit or super well supported yet in existing tools. We're still very early and so much can still be done on the UI/UX of AI assisted coding.

A lot of people quote tweeted this as 1 year anniversary of vibe coding. Some retrospective -

I've had a Twitter account for 17 years now (omg) and I still can't predict my tweet engagement basically at all. This was a shower of thoughts throwaway tweet that I just fired off without thinking but somehow it minted a fitting name at the right moment for something that a lot of people were feeling at the same time, so here we are: vibe coding is now mentioned on my Wikipedia as a major memetic "contribution" and even its article is longer. lol

The one thing I'd add is that at the time, LLM capability was low enough that you'd mostly use vibe coding for fun throwaway projects, demos and explorations. It was good fun and it almost worked. Today (1 year later), programming via LLM agents is increasingly becoming a default workflow for professionals, except with more oversight and scrutiny. The goal is to claim the leverage from the use of agents but without any compromise on the quality of the software. Many people have tried to come up with a better name for this to differentiate it from vibe coding, personally my current favorite "agentic engineering":

- "agentic" because the new default is that you are not writing the code directly 99% of the time, you are orchestrating agents who do and acting as oversight.
- "engineering" to emphasize that there is an art & science and expertise to it. It's something you can learn and become better at, with its own depth of a different kind.

In 2026, we're likely to see continued improvements on both the model layer and the new agent layer. I feel excited about the product of the two and another year of progress.

I attended a vibe coding hackathon recently and used the chance to build a web app (with auth, payments, deploy, etc.). I tinker but I am not a web dev by background, so besides the app, I was very interested in what it's like to vibe code a full web app today. As such, I wrote none of the code directly (Cursor+Claude/o3 did) and I don't really know how the app works, in the conventional sense that I'm used to as an engineer.

The app is called MenuGen, and it is live on https://t.co/bQonQT88t0. Basically I'm often confused about what all the things on a restaurant menu are - e.g. Pâté, Tagine, Cavatappi or Sweetbread (hint it's... not sweet). Enter MenuGen: you take a picture of a menu and it generates images for all the menu items and presents them in a nice list. I find it super useful to get a quick visual sense of the menu.

But the more interesting part for me I thought was the exploration of vibe coding around how easy/hard it is to build and deploy a full web app today if you are not a web developer. So I wrote up the full blog post on my experience here, including some takeaways:
https://t.co/2kkQh0ElgB

Copy pasting just the TLDR:
"Vibe coding menugen was exhilarating and fun escapade as a local demo, but a bit of a painful slog as a deployed, real app. Building a modern app is a bit like assembling IKEA future. There are all these services, docs, API keys, configurations, dev/prod deployments, team and security features, rate limits, pricing tiers... Meanwhile the LLMs have slightly outdated knowledge of everything, they make subtle but critical design mistakes when you watch them closely, and sometimes they hallucinate or gaslight you about solutions. But the most interesting part to me was that I didn't even spend all that much work in the code editor itself. I spent most of it in the browser, moving between tabs and settings and configuring and gluing a monster. All of this work and state is not even accessible or manipulatable by an LLM - how are we supposed to be automating society by 2027 like this?"

See the post for full detail, and maybe give MenuGen a go the next time you're at a restaurant!

Hetzner (and most VPS hosts) automatically disable password login if you add an SSH key during creating the server (usually on their web dashboards)

You should never have password login ever because it can be brute forced (they try 1000s of passwords)

So yes:
- Tailscale firewall subnet for SSH
- Cloudflare firewall subnet for HTTPS (web server)
- disable passwordless login
- enable unattended upgrades and auto reboots (for security updates etc)

My personal one that's fun:
- set the SSH port to some obscure number (I like security by obscurity)

You can install Claude Code and just ask to do all these things

"Creation as a Service" will always beat "Software as a Service". I don't want your lowest-common-denominator software. I want software to work for me and meet my needs. I want platforms and infrastructure.

To me, Slack is a good example of an ideal in-between of SaaS and Platform. I have zero interest in vibe coding my own Slack. (It's also particularly difficult software to perfect and scale, and if you disagree you have no idea how to build software 🤡).

I do have a strong interest in extending Slack with our own agents. As I shared yesterday, more of our company runs on Slack-based "@" agents. We're making it easier than ever to deploy Slack agents on Vercel, with our AI infrastructure (gateway, workflows, sandbox, etc), starting with @v0.

A lot of SaaS might be cooked, but Salesforce has a generational re-birth opportunity with Slack.

Here’s how I think about it:

What has changed in movie production from 1990 when ~6 million people had access to cameras, vs in 2026 when ~6 billion ppl have high res cameras in our pockets?

There are not 10x more amazing movies. But there’s 1,000x more video content out there!

I hadn't thought about this before: unlike regular APIs, MCPs don't need to make any stability promises
"MCP servers are called my LLMs which dynamically read the spec every time, which allow us to constantly change the MCP server. It doesn't matter! We haven't made any promises"

quotedTweet.text:
MCP is mostly nonsense, but there are good effects:

1. Tricks management to green-light engineering efforts on APIs and OAuth DCR (Dynamic Client Registration)

2. Tricks LLM clients to integrate with everyone, ie an open-ecosystem, not walled gardens

3. (Just discovered this one today) The fact that MCP is a difference surface from your normal API allows you to ship MUCH faster to MCP. This has been unlocked by inference at runtime

Normal APIs are promises to developers, because developer commit code that relies on those APIs, and then walk away. If you break the API, you break the promise, and you break that code. This means a developer gets woken up at 2am to fix the code

But MCP servers are called my LLMs which dynamically read the spec every time, which allow us to constantly change the MCP server. It doesn't matter! We haven't made any promises. The LLM can figure it out afresh every time

This is why we've shipped capabilities to our MCP server that our API doesn't yet have. We aren't worried about committing ourselves to anything. We can always change the MCP server later and the LLM will figure it out

candidates.text:
Meta researchers created a mandatory checklist that forces AI to trace code line by line instead of blindly guessing.

This structured approach boosted the accuracy of checking real-world code updates to an impressive 93%.

Usually, when we ask an AI to check if a software update works, it just looks at the names of the functions and makes a very confident guess.

If we want to be absolutely sure the code works, human developers normally have to run the code in expensive and slow testing servers.

This paper changes that dynamic entirely by introducing a strict template that forces the AI to write down the exact path the code takes and provide hard evidence for every single claim it makes.

Because the AI is forced to slow down and show its work step by step, it catches deeply hidden bugs and proves that patches work with 93% accuracy.

The big deal here is that tech companies can now use AI to automatically and reliably verify millions of lines of code without ever paying for the massive computing costs required to actually execute that software.

----

Paper Link – arxiv. org/abs/2603.01896

Paper Title: "Agentic Code Reasoning"

I was telling @DynamicWebPaige about how Google Colab allowed me to use Python SciKit-Learn and notebooks for clustering analysis for the first time.  I was utterly astonished that I got what I needed done within 90 minutes, despite never having used SciKit-Learn, and only having spent ten hours in Jupyter notebooks.

It’s been a decade since I’ve done any Python, and have probably less than 100 hours of Python experience.

For the last 20+ years, I’ve used SPSS as my weapon of choice for statistical analysis.  In the early years of DORA research, almost all our analyses were done in SPSS because Dr. Nicole Forsgren was also a fan of it.

Even SPSS fans will acknowledge that it is the best that the 1970s had to offer — and even now, it is the ideal tool for people doing a certain type of statistical analysis. I kept using it even after using many of the great Python numpy statistical packages in the early 2010s, and exploring R around that timeframe.

I even did a bunch of work in their Syntax programming language, which I think is the actual core of SPSS. If you used in the early 1980s, that’s all you had.  The Java Swing GUI was built upon it much later.  Here’s what that code looks like:

DESCRIPTIVES
VARIABLES=age income
/STATISTICS=MEAN STDDEV MIN MAX.

COMPUTE new_var = age * 2.
EXECUTE.

This is teletype-era UI.  The GUI was merely a wrapper around the Syntax engine, but it still is a marvel to use.

At any rate, I wanted to do some analysis, and I couldn’t get a license key, because I couldn’t log into the IBM e-commerce site.  I never got the email to confirm my account reactivation.

I really needed to get this done that afternoon, and I remembered watching Dr. Stephen Magill in 2018 doing some analysis when we were working together on the State of the Software Supply Chain Report.  He was using SciKit-Learn in a Jupyter notebook, working in a nice REPL-like way, showing off the kickbutt scientific visualizations that the Python ecosystem is so famous for.

I was really envious.

But the idea of getting Python notebooks running on my laptop was daunting — I know I had somehow trashed my Jupyter anaconda something something a year before, and trying to get that running again made me think…  “Maybe next year.”  (I probably have three Python notebook packages installed on my laptop over the last ten years.)

That afternoon, I again pondered what it would take to get notebooks running again on my laptop.  Python3, pip?  Brew?  Venv is a thing, right?  But did I screw that up?  And now there’s uv that everyone is talking about…

As Allison McMillan (@allie_p) said when she was Director of Engineering at GitHub: “It shouldn’t be a core competency to manage two different versions of Python on your machine.”  😆
And apparently, I can’t even get one version of python running on my machine.
Maybe next year.

But I need to get this analysis done!  So how?

Even if I could get Python running, I don’t know any of the libraries — if there were a game called “name that Python library,” I’d rank dead last.  It’s difficult to do something if you don’t know the ecosystem.

But, I’ve been having so much success with Sourcegraph Cody, GitHub Copilot, and Claude, I decided to take the plunge.  Maybe I could get farther than I think!

I've always wanted to try Google Colab for something serious.  A hosted notebook running on Google Cloud?  Awesome.  I always thought it would be to do something GPU related, especially after I spent hours trying to get a VM running with a GPU, and never even got the nvidia driver running correctly (htop or whatever never worked).

So I log into Colab, and amazingly, when you create a code block, it says, “Start coding or generate with AI.”

Holy cow, there’s a coding assistant in Colab!

First prompt: “I have a CSV file I'd like to analyze. how do I upload a file?”  😂
 
See screenshot.

That is amazing!  I think that would have taken me 30m to figure out — if I’m honest, maybe 90m.

Next prompts are:

how many rows?
what are column names of df

Okay, I’m analyzing all the tens of thousands of people who have attended the Enterprise Technology Leadership Summit for the last decade — there are 24 events, but they’re coded like “Las Vegas 2024,” “Amsterdam 2023,” “Virtual 2020,” …

Hmm…  How do I extract the years into an ordinal value, or for that matter, what is the ideal way to even code these columns?  In fact, I’m not sure how I’d do it if I were using SPSS…  I’m getting a paralyzed thinking of the ways I could do this.

Let’s ask Gemini — prompt is something like:

I need columns that indicate they attended in a given year, so I can express ideas like “after 2020 pandemic,” and express recency and frequency.

Looking the code it generated, I’m a bit in awe, because I’m sure it would have taken hours (days?) to figure this out: how Python does regular expressions, how to do mappings from “yes/no” to booleans, numeric conversions, sets, etc..

Seriously, looking at the code, I realize how hopeless this would have been solo.  I have no idea how null (or do they call it nils?) are handled in dicts.  I’ve used panda dataframes once, but primarily what I know about them was watching Magill doing stuff years ago.  

And I don’t exactly remember what the indentation rules are for Python.

Seriously, this would have been hopeless without an LLM assist.

Gemini was a huge boost — it got me so much further than I ever thought I’d get.  I’m generating histograms, boxplots, exactly as I would in SPSS.

X-axis titles getting all squished?  Complain to Gemini, and it fixed it.  (After a whole bunch of “slot machine lever” pulls.  But eventually, I got fantastic graphs.)

Wait, why are 90% of the data points in one cluster?  “Make five clusters!”  “Make 10 clusters!” “Show descriptive stats on each cluster”.

Wait, why are the years all wrong?  Ohhh…. I totally forgot the first critical step — visualize the descriptive stats!  Hey, all the years are zero!  Oops.

I had to go back to the column creation code, because some of the years weren’t being extracted correctly.  I’m about 50m into this exercise, but this is all pretty amazing.  When I feel like I’m going in circles, I begin to wonder if this was all a big mistake and waste of time.

Then I remember that I saw Dr. @DanielRock do something amazing — was he using GitHub Copilot in his notebook?  Oh, duh.  I started copying/pasting code to and from Claude, which definitely picked up the pace.

(Claude is so good at coding.  And I’m excited to see that Gemini 2.0 Flash apparently is even better than Calude!)

I eventually get the year extraction working — I’m guessing it took about seven iterations with Claude to get the correct year extraction.  Something else took about seven iterations to get right — I can’t remember what it was, but it seemed important at the time.

There’s no way I could have done it without Colab and Claude.

This is all freaking magic.

My contribution to the coding?  Typing in one character in a regular expression: “^”.  Because I knew something that Gemini and Claude didn’t — that the year always is at the beginning of the event name.  Which allowed me to do something that was important at the time, but I can’t remember what it was, either.
So, ninety minutes to do a clustering analysis, in a programming language I basically didn’t know, using the amazing but massive pandas dataframe infrastructure (which I’ve really only seen people use, never used myself), scikit-learn, etc.

Without having to figure out how to install python or having to reimage my laptop!

Colab was utterly dependable.  Having Gemini in there is brilliant — although I feel like it’s a couple of generations behind, but it was definitely enough to get me going!

In fact, if that weren’t there, I would have surely given up within 5 minutes.

Okay, gotta turn in for the night.  Thank you Colab team!!!

cc @thechrisperry @GoogleColab @matvelloso

You microservices work fine with 3 services

But what happens when you have 300?

In theory, it is easy:  
Split your big app into small, separate services. Deploy them on their own. Done!

But in real use, it gets messy:  

- How do you set up more than 50 services without stress?  
- What if one fails and stops the rest?  
- How do they connect to each other?  
- How do you grow just one service?  
- How do you update without stopping everything?

This is where Kubernetes helps. It manages containers and runs your services automatically.

It does these things:  

✅ Starts failed services again by itself (no need to worry at night)  
✅ Grows or shrinks based on how busy it is  
✅ Shares traffic evenly  
✅ Helps services find each other  
✅ Updates without any stop time  
✅ Handles settings and secret info safely  
✅ Keeps things running all the time  

Without Kubernetes, you have to do it all by hand. It is risky and confusing.

That is why in job talks, if you say "I used microservices," they ask next:  
"How did you manage them?"  

The answer they want is Kubernetes.  

If you skip this, your microservices skills are not full. Time to learn more.

I'm continually blown away by the engineering and infrastructure excellence needed for these increasingly expensive AI training runs.

(Many have written that these training runs are now routinely exceeding $100MM — Meta will have 500K "H100 compute equivalents" by the end of 2024, which includes 340K actual H100s.)

But even getting to scarce GPUs is no guarantee of success.

To get a sense of the challenges, here's some highlights from  @swyx's interview with @joshalbrecht, CTO of Imbue, and @jefrankle, Chief AI Scientist of Databricks.

Josh described the infrastructure engineering required to get 4000+ H100s built out and running usefully in the face of idiosyncracies and random failures — which, at these scales, happen all the time.

Some stats:

- GPU failure rate: 3% of machines break every week, leading to potential turnover of entire clusters within a year
- InfiniBand cables were routinely stolen by "porch pirates," because they're in such short supply
- 12,000 cables connect the 4,000 GPUs in Imbue's cluster
- @imbue_ai infrastructure team: 3-6 people, depending on how many things are going wrong
- lots of tooling was built out to provision systems, diagnose failures, roll out fixes (sometimes rebooting made problems much worse, not better)
- GPU and other drivers routinely had problems (from NVIDIA,  DELL etc), and had to be rolled out

- Portions were used to create DBRx model: 132 billion parameters, 36 billion active parameters, trained on 12 trillion tokens of text and code.

I suspect DevOps infrastructure and operations people will find this interview very interesting and entertaining, as the AI field rediscovers the lessons of the last 20 years in building and running high-performance computing and planet-scale infrastructure.

A day in the life of building/maintaining these systems:

- Even when using a cloud provider, you're still having to ask them about firmware drivers (and pester them to push out updates), which InfiniBand cables they're using, and even power contracts!
- this motivated @imbue_ai to build their cluster locally, to have more control — but involved having to wait for InfiniBand cables at the loading dock, so they won't get stolen

quotedTweet.text:
A Vercel user reported an issue that sounded extremely scary. An unknown GitHub OSS codebase being deployed to their team.

We, of course, took the report extremely seriously and began an investigation. Security and infra engineering engaged.

Turns out Opus 4.6 *hallucinated a public repository ID* and used our API to deploy it. Luckily for this user, the repository was harmless and random. The JSON payload looked like this:

    "𝚐𝚒𝚝𝚂𝚘𝚞𝚛𝚌𝚎": {                                                                                                                                    
      "𝚝𝚢𝚙𝚎": "𝚐𝚒𝚝𝚑𝚞𝚋",                                                                                                                               
      "𝚛𝚎𝚙𝚘𝙸𝚍": "𝟿𝟷𝟹𝟿𝟹𝟿𝟺𝟶𝟷",  // ⚠️ 𝚑𝚊𝚕𝚕𝚞𝚌𝚒𝚗𝚊𝚝𝚎𝚍
      "𝚛𝚎𝚏": "𝚖𝚊𝚒𝚗"                                                                                                                                   
    }

When the user asked the agent to explain the failure, it confessed:

The agent never looked up the GitHub repo ID via the GitHub API. There are zero GitHub API calls in the session before the first rogue deployment.

The number 913939401 appears for the first time at line 877 — the agent fabricated it entirely.

The agent knew the correct project ID (prj_▒▒▒▒▒▒) and project name (▒▒▒▒▒▒) but invented a plausible-looking numeric repo ID rather than looking it up.

Some takeaways:
▪️ Even the smartest models have bizarre failure modes that are very different from ours. Humans make lots of mistakes, but certainly not make up a random repo id.
▪️ Powerful APIs create additional risks for agents. The API exist to import and deploy legitimate code, but not if the agent decides to hallucinate what code to deploy!
▪️ Thus, it's likely the agent would have had better results had it not decided to use the API and stuck with CLI or MCP.

This reinforces our commitment to make Vercel the most secure platform for agentic engineering. Through deeper integrations with tools like Claude Code and additional guardrails, we're confident security and privacy will be upheld.

Note: the repo id above is randomized for privacy reasons.

Christoph describes live sports production as "capturing audio-visual data, putting it all together into a big live soup of information and video and audio and experience, and then sending that out to screens and streams and the national airwaves."

"We're pulling in video, mics, and we've got microphones and cameras. We're going behind the scenes, and we're getting little featurettes of audio and video of that.

He shows pictures of the massive endeavor this kind of production requires. He shows the broadcasting control room (which is super super cool), "you can see all these sources coming in live. You have stuff that's recorded that you're going to play out later and things like that.

There are so many data sources!  For starters, there’s the score, clock, map, team names, team location (which side are they on)…

"We have the match score, well that's data. And that's from a totally different place, it's not even from the game. It's from some tournament system. We have what this match means, this is a championship match, so that's some context, well that's data too.

"We have the players, we have the order they're sitting in. We have to match the players up to the seats because we have cameras on all the players.

"We need to know when the producer presses, you know, button three, which player that's going to be. We also have their jersey numbers and more information about that. We have whether they're alive or dead or have special powers in the game.

"We even have a feed of notable events that have occurred. So you can see even in this one screenshot that didn't seem like much when we first put it on, there's data everywhere. Right? And then that isn't even counting all of the gameplay statistics."

And all of this enables the producers to tell a story — by combining video, game information, and data to create a "live act of production."

"So, all of those sources come together to create a story. We're taking the video, we're taking the game, we're taking the data, we're putting it all together, and we're telling a story of what you're seeing, and why it matters.

"And the talent, or the casters and the commentators, they're telling that story. It's a team effort. So we take those audio and video sources, we bring them all together, we throw some data on top, and we tell this story. We create this experience.

"Whether you're in the venue, and you're seeing things on the big screen, or you're outside and you're watching the broadcast, we're weaving a story of what you're seeing and why it matters, using all that. And it is a live act of production. It's going to happen whether you make a mistake or not. It's going to happen even if something crashes or goes down. You're going to have to do it, and you're going to have to keep going."

"The bigger the game, the more complex the production, but the more complex the production, the higher the stakes. And so, you have this really demanding, fast-paced, dynamic environment where Clojure comes in, and it shines."

Clare talked about more unexpected benefits: "This improved understanding of our roadmap had a surprising benefit. It actually allowed us to capitalize more software."

"For years we had been expensing infrastructure investments without realizing that we could capitalize them."

He distinguishes between two modes of using AI programming tools: exploratory prototyping and production code writing.

In exploratory mode, he uses AI to quickly experiment with different options and generate prototypes, even in languages he doesn't know.

When working with production code, he treats the AI tool like a fast, talent, but very overconfident intern who needs to be constantly corrected, who never gets tired or complains, allowing him to work late at night without interruption.

There remains a high need for human oversight and intervention.

Takeaways:

- Building AI models require an insane level of infrastructure engineering, and a huge degree of specialization in the more archane aspects of exotic hardware, firmware, etc.
- The job of these infrastructure engineers to enable the AI model researchers to achieve their goals, without getting mired down in the details of power contracts, tracking down InfiniBand cables, etc.
- It is an enormous undertaking to keep these massive clusters running — they are probably rediscovering hard-won lessons of running planet-scale distributed systems.

This is such a great example of increasing functional specializing happening — you simply can't be an expert in all the things (ex: AI models and power contracts)

PS: The consequences of this is something we explored heavily in our "Wiring the Winning Organization" book — the more functional specialties you have, the more sophisticated the organizational wiring must be:

- must create better abstraction and modularity
- must ensure clarity on who does what, and when
